item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: for some reason the imagery of a bunch of naked people with their feet stuck in the dirt scares me worse than if they were just creepy half-human half-tree people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the screams were louder for the woman, I was hoping we'd see an autopsy find that she was "pregnant" with a sapling or something; some reason for the loudness other than 'oh no not again'.

Otherwise, I +1, because unlike other folks here, I don't believe the only people who get to write Series I type articles are the people who wrote Series I. This fits right into the world for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's the aftermath of uprooting them that sells this to me; the skip itself is creepy and solidly written but not exceptional, but I'm loving that we're basically left with, "We're pretty sure this guy crawled off into a corner to die somewhere but we've got better things do to than actually, you know, spend a lot of time looking for him. Definitely still tell someone if you manage to find his decaying, dirt-filled corpse, though." Especially with the implication that this is somewhere in Aokigahara - what's one more anonymous body moldering in the underbrush going to matter, really?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What kind of "agriculture research outpost" has armed guards patrolling the exterior of the building? It sounds like these things cause no compulsion to get people to join them, so I doubt that's really necessary. The guards probably cause more questions than they deter.

The idea was that it was supposedly a corporate outpost with security guards. I may change it to them being armed with tasers or something though.

Although I do have an origin in mind for this skip, there's nothing really to "get" about it; nothing that any backstory would add to anyway. Honestly, it's probably better as a mystery than what I have in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. I got what you were tring to do, but it wasn't particularly interesting. I also thought a lot of the redactions were unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the article:

In one superstition, people who pull up this root will be condemned to hell, and the mandrake root would scream as it was pulled from the ground, killing anyone who heard it. Therefore, in the past, people have tied the roots to the bodies of animals and then used these animals to pull the roots from the soil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation is an international organization, so it uses both spellings, usually assumed to be a reflection of whoever wrote it in-universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes the armchair psychologist in me very happy to see someone unintentionally recreate an archetypical trope like the mandrake. Knowing that, the similarities of your convergently evolved form are as fascinating as the differences, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I was really meaning mandrake as a mythical being, although thanks for the info.

Mandrakes are usually depicted as magical plants that have human bodies instead of roots under the surface. When uprooted, they scream, which usually has some form of negative effects. They appear in the harry potter series, and probably a few others I've read, although I can't remember.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think about that. The site usually uses American spelling, so I'll change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it supposed to be storey or story? I only ask because I'm not familiar with SCP canon, if we're assuming the site is American based it should be changed to story, but I wanted to check before I go ruining a perfectly good scp entry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I enjoy this. It's not as "Oh-my-god-I'm-gonna-die" as some of the scips out there, but it's not as if you can just leave them in a safe box for years and nothing will happen. It would be useful if some tests were done, say trying soil transfers, pouring water on their heads, ect. I want to see how plantlike they get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate everyone's comments on my first SCP article. A lot of people are saying I should include a backstory/origin, but I'd prefer to keep things mysterious.

All I will say is: They are not plant-people, they are people who think they are plants. I may include a paragraph that hints at this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Redacting the forest name was done simply because I've seen it done in other articles where the place name (or other censored name) was obvious. I like the balance of 99% certainty, 1% doubt that it creates. The location itself is not that important. The Site description is in the SCP section because part of it is necessary to the upkeep of SCP-2157 (the natural floor and retractable roof), and I figured why not put the whole layout in there rather than divide it up.

Edit: As for the doctor's informal comment, I deliberately made that a researcher's note precisely so I could write it informally, as I felt there was no reason/way to put it in the formal article, but felt it still deserved calling attention to. If that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid I don't know what mandrakes are. As for the researchers, three were killed, one is… missing, but they were not turned into other instances of SCP-2157.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ten-letter forest name that's redacted and in Japan, with creepy subject matter in the article? So… it's Aokigahara, right? Was that supposed to be easy to figure out, or is it not really a major part of the article? (Or I'm wrong about the name, but the odds seem slim when it comes to character count.)

I'm neutral-voting. It's unsettling, but it lacks an explanation or backstory. Sure, skips don't always need that, but this one isn't gripping enough to excuse it for me personally. Beyond that, some of the syntax and diction is really toeing the line between casual and clinical, particularly in Dr. M██████'s comment. Also, the way the second paragraph of the containment procedures is written makes it sound like it should be in the description, even though I know it shouldn't be because it's describing the Site. Not sure how to fix that, or if it even needs fixing, but I thought I'd mention it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mandrakes. Obviously mandrakes, but a nice SCP take on it. I appreciate how the scream doesn't immediately kill people. I take that the researchers are turned into replacement mandrakes, but because of today's urban environment, they were instantly considered uprooted and killed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing everything here. Competently written but just not that interesting. I'm also of a mind that explaining where it comes from or what happened to the researcher would not help. The mystery is basically the only thing the article has going right now.

I'm not sure about my vote. Novote for now, may lean into downvote upon reconsideration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So they're plant people? It's an original idea, but I didn't get too much out of the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't mind the fact that there wasn't a reason for the anomaly, since the anomaly itself was pretty interesting. However, while I do like the "weird thing with no explanation" thing this has going on, which kinda gives it a Series I feel, there is something lacking. It might have been that the imagery didn't hit me hard enough, or perhaps that the ending was rather weak, but there's something missing that could have made me upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those scenarios where it shows that idea matters. The execution is very good. But the idea and where you took it wasn't interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
forty-three (43), forty-one (41)

Just go with one or the otherrrr, the whole "number (#)" thing doesn't work in this instance.

This didn't grab me, and though it's competently-written, I found the narrative being told boring. I'll give it a read some other time, but I don't think this really appeals to me on any sort of level. You don't even describe what it actually does or how it affects people until the addenda; you just state how odd it is and weird, and then build the story from there.

The one thing it has going for it that I liked was how their feet are in the soil, and the soil isn't anomalous and they aren't physically connected, yet removing them from the soil is fatal. It was certainly a weird touch and added a bit of imagery, but the rest didn't go anywhere for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'm with Dr N. on this one. It's quite good, well-written and pleasantly devoid of unnecessary fluff, but it does feel as if a bit of polish would really bring out the shine…

Ideas/thoughts:

  • Perhaps a bit more of a hint as to why they're there, why they're facing west, or what their connection to the forest/earth is.
  • Give the readers a hint as to what happened to the missing researcher; why did that one vanish when the other ones died? Even a vague justification would give the reader a better sense of what these things are and if they're inherently hostile, indifferent or beneficent (as long as nobody, y'know, hurts them).
  • Perhaps a reference to a local legend, an ancient myth or something like that would help.

Now, I'm not saying "everything needs a deep backstory with lots of supplements". Again, some of the best articles on the site are short-form. But, if you want to leave the reader with a creeping sense of discomfort or horror, a few pointedly unanswered questions are better than a huge, vague mystery without any points to really grab onto… Which is where we're currently at, I feel, and is why the good Dr. and I probably both feel that hard-to-define sense of "good, but needs that extra somethin'".

Anyway, that's my two cents. Still quite good, regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. My only complaint is that rather than implying the last researcher's body was just never found, you could possibly intergate him/her into the skip somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool and creepy. One thing I will say, though, is that the method of death reminded me a lot of SCP-1609, though that's not necessarily a bad thing. I just feel it could use a bit more substance, that's all. Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral… for now.

I'm not sure what to think of this one. I like it. But I'm not sure what to think yet. Maybe that's part of it… weird for weird's sake? That'd be okay. But I feel like there is a deeper meaning here, and I want to figure it out before I vote, so I will be reading this again soon.

EDIT: So I reread this, and I'm afraid I have to remain neutral. I don't understand what's happening here… and when I know I don't understand an article, I vote neutral. There's a hook, what with them being siblings, with the deaths, with what you said about them thinking their plants, but either I'm too dense to understand it, or it's not clear enough. I would suggest you add a tiny little bit more of a nudge into what's happening here in the skip itself, because as it stands, I'm not sure anyone here gets it. If not, that's fine, I still think its cool and creepy and mysterious. Just one thing I've been dying to ask though,

Site-49 has been constructed around SCP-2157 for the purposes of containing and studying it under the public pretense of an agriculture research outpost. Guards armed with light weapons are to patrol the exterior of the site and deter civilian approach.

What kind of "agriculture research outpost" has armed guards patrolling the exterior of the building? It sounds like these things cause no compulsion to get people to join them, so I doubt that's really necessary. The guards probably cause more questions than they deter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha! I had forgotten all about that. It must have been a latent influence though, because for some reason in my head it had to be in Japan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP reminds me of the "Japanese garden" in Police Squad and develops the concept. Brought a smile to my face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only one researcher disappears, the rest die. I appreciate your input and upvote though, thanks!

I get what you mean about the ending. Believe it or not, this is actually "punchier" than my original idea, which just sort fizzled out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really grabbed at the hook on this one. The description was really vivid and creepy at the same time. Overall, it's quite a good piece. My only nitpick was that I felt it was a bit lacking at the end. Four researchers disappears, and that's it. This article could've benefited with a deeper ending.

+1
